ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai coconut ice cream; closest defensible match is the generic ice cream food product class. Coconut-milk base and Thai toppings are not individually represented.
ontology.foodex2_description: Ice cream and similar frozen desserts
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW covers ice cream and similar frozen desserts; no specific code for coconut-milk-based Thai ice cream exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Thai coconut ice cream; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical street-food serving of Thai coconut ice cream is approximately 150 g, served in a cup or on a bun with a selection of toppings such as roasted peanuts, palm seeds, sweet corn, sticky rice, and taro.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Coconut Ice Cream — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Thai coconut ice cream with toppings (cr_id F02020014). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from coconut milk/cream composition using USDA FoodData Central coconut milk data (USDA SR Legacy, NDB 12117) as a supplementary reference, since HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item.

gi_source.title: Coconut milk ice cream — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for ice cream (full-fat, regular) and coconut milk composition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai coconut ice cream with toppings in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate derived from: (1) full-fat dairy ice cream GI ~61 (University of Sydney GI Database); (2) coconut milk's lower glycaemic impact relative to dairy due to high fat content and lower lactose; (3) the mixed toppings (sticky rice, sweet corn, taro) which elevate the composite GI above pure coconut ice cream. The input record cited GI 57 at confidence 0.375 from an embedded GI database; this value is retained as a defensible medium-GI estimate consistent with the analogous evidence. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 8.55) / 100 ≈ 4.9 for a 150 g serving (carbohydrate 5.7 g/100 g × 1.5 = 8.55 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk (~7.8 g/100 g) contributing to arterial stiffness; sodium is low at 64 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less due to the high saturated fat content from coconut milk and cream, which contributes to arterial stiffness; sodium is low and is not the primary concern for this food.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (57) with high-GI toppings (sticky rice, taro, sweet corn) elevating composite glycaemic load; carbohydrate 5.7 g/100 g but typical serving with toppings delivers significant starch load
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should choose minimal sticky rice and taro toppings, prioritise roasted peanuts as the lowest-GI topping, and limit portion to 100 g or less to manage postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at ~7.8 g/100 g from coconut milk and cream, substantially exceeding the 5 g/100 g threshold associated with elevated LDL-cholesterol risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of Thai coconut ice cream due to its high saturated fat content (~7.8 g/100 g) from coconut milk and cream, which raises LDL-cholesterol; occasional small portions (≤100 g) may be tolerated within an otherwise heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~7.8 g/100 g from coconut milk promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to RA pathophysiology
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular consumption of this dish as the high coconut-derived saturated fat (~7.8 g/100 g) promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines; roasted peanuts provide minor anti-inflammatory benefit but are insufficient to offset the pro-inflammatory fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai coconut ice cream with toppings (ไอศกรีมกะทิ) is a popular Thai street dessert made by churning and freezing a base of coconut milk and coconut cream with sugar, producing a rich, creamy, dairy-free frozen confection. The ice cream is characteristically dense and intensely coconut-flavoured, with a smooth texture that is slightly firmer than dairy ice cream due to the higher fat content of coconut cream. It is traditionally served in a cup or on a small bun and topped with a selection of garnishes including roasted peanuts, palm seeds (attap chee), sweet corn, sticky rice, and taro. The combination of the cold, creamy coconut base with the varied textures of the toppings — crunchy peanuts, chewy sticky rice, soft taro, and sweet corn — is central to the dish's appeal. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 115 kcal, 9.23 g fat (predominantly saturated fat ~7.8 g from coconut), 5.7 g carbohydrate, 2.4 g protein, 1.7 g dietary fibre, and 64 mg sodium. The high saturated fat content from coconut milk is the primary clinical concern, making the dish unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with cardiovascular disease, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis. The medium glycaemic index (~57) and the high-GI toppings (sticky rice, taro, sweet corn) make portion control important for people with type 2 diabetes. The dish is plant-based, dairy-free, and halal-suitable, making it accessible to a wide range of dietary preferences in Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 (cr_id F02020014). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy coconut milk data as a supplementary reference since HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, consistent with the input record value of 57.
notes: potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for Thai coconut ice cream with toppings are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F02020014) or USDA FoodData Central for this composite dish as of 2026. Saturated fat (7.8 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.4 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.2 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy coconut milk fatty acid profile (NDB 12117) applied proportionally to the total fat of 9.23 g/100 g. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for plant-based coconut ice cream; no trans fats in coconut milk per USDA SR Legacy). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero: plant-based coconut milk contains no cholesterol per USDA SR Legacy NDB 12117). GI of 57 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database full-fat ice cream data; direct measurement for Thai coconut ice cream is not available. Dietary tags include plant_based, low_sodium, and low_calorie as per input; note that the high saturated fat content means this food is not clinically low-risk despite being plant-based.
